frozen_components: latent reasoning backbone (COCONUT, CODI, CoLaR, Latent-SFT, RoT)
architecture.summary: A parallel test-time scaling framework for latent reasoning models that operates in continuous hidden-state space rather than token space. The system generates multiple stochastic latent reasoning trajectories via two sampling methods (Monte Carlo Dropout or Additive Gaussian Noise applied to intermediate hidden states), then aggregates them using a learned Latent Reward Model (LatentRM) that scores each latent thought via step-wise contrastive supervision. Information flow: input → stochastic latent trajectory generation (multiple samples) → LatentRM scoring of intermediate steps → trajectory selection (best-of-N or beam search) → final answer decoding.

biblio.title: Parallel Test-Time Scaling for Latent Reasoning Models

claims.statement: Both Monte Carlo Dropout and Additive Gaussian Noise enable parallel test-time scaling for latent reasoning models by introducing controlled stochasticity into continuous latent space, addressing the absence of sampling mechanisms and probabilistic signals in latent models.
MC-dropout consistently achieves higher coverage across nearly all sample sizes, with coverage increasing monotonically as sample count increases, showing saturation effects at larger N.
At N=64 samples, COCONUT and CODI achieve nearly equivalent coverage despite CODI's clear superiority at N=1, indicating that increased sampling narrows inter-model performance gaps.
Best-of-N selection and Beam Search guided by LatentRM consistently outperform Majority Voting across compute budgets, with gains most pronounced on MultiArith.
On RoT-4B with MATH500, the framework improves from 20.3% to 22.0% accuracy with N=16 sampled trajectories.

Monte Carlo Dropout produces directional drift with dense contiguous exploration, while Additive Gaussian Noise yields isotropic radial dispersion; MC-dropout excels on harder tasks by larger displacement toward distant correct regions, whereas AGN maintains robustness by preserving probability mass near the deterministic center.

Ablation on LatentRM shows step-wise contrastive loss contributes 1.9% improvement over BCE on GSM-Test and 0.4% on GSM-Hard; removing stochastic rollouts causes further 2.8% and 1.4% drops respectively; random untrained head performs below Majority Voting.

Latent reasoning combined with parallel TTS demonstrates superior efficiency compared to explicit Chain-of-Thought, achieving competitive performance with significantly fewer decoding steps while maintaining wall-clock latency advantages on GPU inference.
